name: thermochem

metadata.name: thermochem
metadata.summary: Python utilities for thermodynamics and thermochemistry

documentation:
# Thermochem

```{image} https://github.com/adelq/thermochem/actions/workflows/package-tests.yml/badge.svg
:target: https://github.com/adelq/thermochem/actions/workflows/package-tests.yml?query=branch%3Amaster
```

```{image} https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/thermochem.svg?maxAge=2592000
:target: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/thermochem
```

```{image} https://readthedocs.org/projects/thermochem/badge/?version=latest
:alt: Documentation Status
:target: http://thermochem.readthedocs.io/en/latest/?badge=latest
```

Thermochem is a package with some useful modules for Thermodynamics
and Thermochemistry. The following tools are provided:

- IAPWS data and functions to compute the thermodynamic properties of
  water an steam.
- Alexander Burcat's database and routines to compute the properties
  of more than 1300 substances from 200K to 6000K.
- JANAF thermodynamic data pulled from NIST website to compute
  properties of more than 1200 substances from 0K to 2500K.
- Simple reactor for modeling combustion of single organic compounds.
- Moist gas model.

## Requirements

- Python 2.7 or Python 3.5+
- Linux, Windows, MacOS, BSD, and any other platform with Python support and can
  install the required dependencies.

## Install

The easy and quick way:

```
$ pip install thermochem
```

For more details and alternate installation instructions, see the [installation
instructions](http://thermochem.readthedocs.io/en/stable/install.html).

## Dependencies

This packages depends on the following modules to run. These should be installed
automatically with any of the installation instructions provided.

- NumPy >= 1.2.1
- SciPy >= 0.6.0
- pandas >= 0.17.0

## Development

If you want to further develop thermochem you must install pytest for testing.

## License

Thermochem is licensed under the BSD license.

functions.name: thermochem.combustion.balance
documentation:
Function that balances the combustion equation given any simple
fuel element

:fuel:

  Formula of a Burcat's database fuel as a string. The fuel can
  only contain C, H, O and N.  Other atoms will be ignored.

:phi:

  Equivalence ratio for air.
TODO: Check that adiabatic flame temperature is ok

functions.name: thermochem.combustion.balance_mix
documentation:
function that balances the combustion equation given a mix of
fuels.

:fuels:

  type Mixture. Simple fuels formed only by C, H, O and N and the
  amount of each one

:phi:

  Equivalence ratio for air.

classes.name: thermochem.combustion.SimpleCombustor

methods.name: thermochem.combustion.SimpleCombustor.__init__
documentation:
---
db: |-
  Element database.  It is passed as argument because it has
  to be allocated only once.
fuel: The formula of the fuel
phi: Equivalence ratio
---

```pycon
>>> from .burcat import Elementdb
>>> db = Elementdb()
>>> methane = db.getelementdata("CH4   RRHO")
>>> combustor = SimpleCombustor(methane,1,db)
>>> print(round(combustor.products.cp, 6))
1112.222587
>>> print(round(combustor.heat_of_comb(298.15), 2))
50027136.34
>>> print(round(combustor.adiabatic_flame_temp(298.15)[0], 6))
2128.632842
```

methods.name: thermochem.combustion.SimpleCombustor.heat_of_comb
documentation: Calculates the heat of combustion per kg of fuel. Checked ok

methods.name: thermochem.combustion.SimpleCombustor.adiabatic_flame_temp
documentation:
This is the adiabatic flame temp for the given mixtures of
reactants and products.  If you want the true adiabatic flame
temperature remember to set the equivalence ratio to 1.
Otherwise you will always get lower temperatures.

This class models a simple combustor that uses fuel as a reductor
and air as a single oxidizer. The combustion is complete, no CO
nor radicals are formed.

It only supports fuels with C, N, H and O.  If you put a more
complicated fuel it will ignore the rest of atoms to balance the
reaction.

classes.name: thermochem.psicrometry.MoistAir

methods.name: thermochem.psicrometry.MoistAir.phi
documentation: Relative moisture given pressure and temperature.

methods.name: thermochem.psicrometry.MoistAir.wet_bulb_T
documentation: Wet bulb temperature for a given pressure

Class that models a moist gas.  The computations in this class are
a bit tricky because the enthalpy computations for steam far from
atmospheric pressure using the bcura data have severe
deviations. Considering water an ideal gas is a too strong
assumption. This means that water data is computed using the IAPWS
data for water an steam.

The trickiest part comes with the fact that the enthalpy reference
for the IAPWS tables is the water's triple point when the enthalpy
reference for the bcura tables is the absolute zero.

The IAPWS reference is the leading one.

MoistAir does not inherit from Mixture because of this.

classes.name: thermochem.iapws.Water

methods.name: thermochem.iapws.Water.psat
documentation:
Returns the saturation pressure of water at a given temperature.

Remember that temperature must be between 273.15K (triple point)
and 647.096K (critical point)

Temperatures in K, Pressures in Pa.

```pycon
>>> w = Water()
>>> w.psat(300)
3536.5894130130105
>>> w.psat(130)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python2.5/doctest.py", line 1228, in __run
    compileflags, 1) in test.globs
  File "<doctest __main__.Water.psat[2]>", line 1, in <module>
    w.psat(130)
  File "iapws.py", line 153, in psat
    'No saturation pressure for this temperature')
ValueError: No saturation pressure for this temperature
>>> w.psat(700)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python2.5/doctest.py", line 1228, in __run
    compileflags, 1) in test.globs
  File "<doctest __main__.Water.psat[3]>", line 1, in <module>
     w.psat(700)
  File "iapws.py", line 146, in psat
    'No saturation pressure for this temperature')
ValueError: No saturation pressure for this temperature
```

methods.name: thermochem.iapws.Water.Tsat
documentation:
Returns the saturation temperature of water at a given pressure.

Remember that pressure must be between 0.000611213MPa (triple
point) and 22.064MPa (critical point)

Temperatures in K, pressures in MPa.

```pycon
>>> w = Water()
>>> w.Tsat(100000)
372.7559186113...
>>> w.Tsat(1200000)
461.1146416213...
>>> w.Tsat(100)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python2.5/doctest.py", line 1228, in __run
    compileflags, 1) in test.globs
  File "<doctest __main__.Water.Tsat[3]>", line 1, in <module>
    w.Tsat(100)
  File "iapws.py", line 193, in Tsat
    raise ValueError('No saturation temperature for this pressure')
ValueError: No saturation temperature for this pressure
>>> w.Tsat(101325)
373.12430000048056
```

methods.name: thermochem.iapws.Water.h
documentation:
Returns specific enthalpy (J/kg) for a given pressure (Pa) and
Temperature (K).

```pycon
>>> w = Water()
>>> round(w.h(3000000,300), 6)
115.331273
>>> w.h(3500,300)
2549.9114508400203
```

There are also error codes

Results checked against the reference.

methods.name: thermochem.iapws.Water.T_ph
documentation:
Returns the temperature (K) given the pressure (MPa) and
specific enthalpy (kJ/kg).  Only region 2a implemented
(p\<4MPa) (Reimplement).

```pycon
>>> w = Water()
>>> w.T_ph(3,500)[0]
391.798...
>>> w.T_ph(3,500)[1]
4.1313...e+21
```

modules.name: thermochem.codata
documentation:
Fundamental Physical Constants

These constants are taken from CODATA Recommended Values of the
Fundamental Physical Constants: 2002. They may be found at
physics.nist.gov/constants. The values are stored in the dictionary
physical_constants as a tuple containing the value, the units, and
the relative precision, in that order. All constants are in SI units
unless otherwise stated.

Several helper functions are provided:

value(key) returns the value of the physical constant.
unit(key) returns the units of the physical constant.
precision(key) returns the relative precision of the physical constant.
find(sub) prints out a list of keys containing the string sub.
Compiled by Charles Harris
Taken from his email message to scipy-dev
dated October 3, 2002
updated to 2002 values by BasSw, 2006

functions.name: thermochem.codata.value
documentation: value indexed by key

functions.name: thermochem.codata.unit
documentation: unit indexed by key

functions.name: thermochem.codata.precision
documentation: relative precision indexed by key

functions.name: thermochem.codata.find
documentation: list all keys containing the string sub

documentation:
This factory makes that any returned value is a measure
instead of a float.

documentation:
Specify temperature units, default is Kelvin.

Unit one of 'K', 'C', or 'F' for Kelvin, Celsius, or Fahrenheit

methods.name: thermochem.units.Temperature.C
documentation: Convert temperature to Celsius

methods.name: thermochem.units.Temperature.F
documentation: Convert temperature to Fahrenheit

Class that models a temperature measure with conversion utilities

Supported units are

- Kelvin
- Celsius
- Fahrenheit

Normal instantiation is a temperature in Kelvin

```pycon
>>> T = Temperature(100)
>>> T
100.0
```

But you can instantiate and specify if unit is Celsius or
Fahrenheit

```pycon
>>> T = Temperature(100).unit('F')
>>> T
310.92777777777775
```

Unit conversion is as easy as it gets.

```pycon
>>> T.C
37.777777777777...
>>> T.F
99.999999999999...
```

You can compute with temperatures because inherits from the float
built-in

```pycon
>>> T1 = Temperature(200)
>>> T2 = Temperature(0).unit('C')
>>> round(T1+T2, 2)
473.15
```

If you don't want to use the class' attribute you can use the
function `getattr` to get a value using the unit code.

```pycon
>>> getattr(T,'C')
37.77777777777...
```

methods.name: thermochem.units.Pressure.torr
documentation: Convert pressure to torrs

Class that models a Pressure measure with conversion utilities

Suported units are

- Pascal (Pa)
- Mega Pascal (MPa)
- Bar (bar)
- Pound per square inch (psi)
- Atmosphere (atm)
- Millimeters of water column (mmwc)
- Torricelli (torr)

Normal instantiation is pressure in Pa. How much is an athmosphere?

```pycon
>>> p = Pressure(1.0).unit('atm')
>>> p
101325.0
>>> p.torr
760.0
>>> p.mmwc
10285.839999999998
>>> p.psi
14.69594877551345
```

modules.name: thermochem.janaf
documentation:
This module gets thermodynamic data from the JANAF database.
Files are downloaded from the NIST servers as needed and then cached locally.

Zack Gainsforth

Funding by NASA

variables.name: thermochem.janaf.Tr
documentation: K

classes.name: thermochem.janaf.JanafPhase
documentation:
Class which is created by Janafdb for a specific phase.

It reads in the JANAF data file and produces functions which interpolate
the thermodynamic constants.

Tr stands for reference temperature and is 298.15 K

```pycon
>>> db = Janafdb()
>>> p = db.getphasedata(formula='O2Ti', name='Rutile', phase='cr')
>>> p.cp([500, 550, 1800]).astype(int).tolist()
[67, 68, 78]
>>> print(p.S([500, 550, 1800]))        # Entropy in J/mol/K
[  82.201    88.4565  176.876 ]
>>> print(p.gef([500, 550, 1800]))      # [G-H(Tr)]/T in J/mol/K
[  57.077   59.704  115.753]
>>> print(p.hef([500, 550, 1800]))      # H-H(Tr) in kJ/mol
[  12.562    15.9955  110.022 ]
>>> print(p.DeltaH([500, 550, 1800]))   # Enthalpy in kJ/mol
[-943670.  -943229.5 -936679. ]
>>> print(p.DeltaG([500, 550, 1800]))   # Gibbs free enegy in kJ/mol
[-852157.  -843046.5 -621013. ]
>>> p.logKf([500, 550, 1800]).astype(int).tolist() # Equilibrium constant of formation.
[89, 80, 18]
>>> print(p.cp(1000))                   # Heat capacity in J/mol/K
74.852
>>> print(p.cp(50000))                  # Example of erroneous extrapolation.
Traceback (most recent call last):
    ...
ValueError: A value in x_new is above the interpolation range.
```

classes.name: thermochem.janaf.Janafdb

methods.name: thermochem.janaf.Janafdb.__init__
documentation:
We have an index file which can be used to build the url for all phases
on the NIST site.

methods.name: thermochem.janaf.Janafdb.search
documentation:
List all the species containing a string. Helpful for
interactive use of the database.

# Parameters

searchstr

: The search string to look for

# Returns

pandas.DataFrame

: Dataframe containing valid phases

# Examples

```pycon
>>> db = Janafdb()
>>> s = db.search('Rb-')
>>> print(s)
     formula           name phase filename
1710     Rb-  Rubidium, Ion     g   Rb-007
>>> s = db.search('Ti')
>>> print(len(s))
88
```

methods.name: thermochem.janaf.Janafdb.getphasedata
documentation:
Returns an element instance given the name of the element.
formula, name and phase match the respective fields in the JANAF index.

# Parameters

formula

: Select records that match the chemical formula

name

: Select records that match the chemical/mineral name

phase

: Select records that match the chemical phase.
  Must be one of the following valid phases:
  cr, l, cr,l, g, ref, cd, fl, am, vit, mon, pol, sln, aq, sat

filename

: Select only records that match the filename on the website, which
  is very unique.

cache

: Whether to cache the Janaf database. Setting this to false will
  download the Janaf database every time it is used.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> db = Janafdb()
>>> db.getphasedata(formula='O2Ti', phase='cr')
Traceback (most recent call last):
    ...
ValueError: There are 2 records matching this pattern:
    ...
Please select a unique record.
>>> db.getphasedata(formula='Oxyz')
Traceback (most recent call last):
    ...
ValueError: Did not find a phase with formula = Oxyz
            Please provide enough information to select a unique record.
            Also check that you didn't eliminate the record you want by choosing too many constraints where one or more constraint is incorrect.
>>> db.getphasedata(formula='Oxyz', phase='l')
Traceback (most recent call last):
    ...
ValueError: Did not find a phase with formula = Oxyz, phase = l
            Please provide enough information to select a unique record.
            Also check that you didn't eliminate the record you want by choosing too many constraints where one or more constraint is incorrect.
>>> FeO = db.getphasedata(formula='FeO', phase='cr,l')
>>> print(FeO)
<thermochem.janaf.JanafPhase object at 0x...>
  Iron Oxide (FeO)  Fe1O1(cr,l)
    Cp(298.15) = 49.915 J/mol/K
    S(298.15) = 60.752 J/mol/K
    [G-H(298.15)]/298.15 = 60.752 J/mol/K
    H-H(298.15) = 0.000 J/mol/K
    Delta_fH(298.15) = -272044 J/mol
    Delta_fG(298.15) = -251429 J/mol
    log(Kf((298.15)) = 44.049
```

Class that reads the NIST JANAF tables for thermodynamic data.

Data is initially read from the web servers, and then cached.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> rutile = Janafdb().getphasedata(name='Rutile')
```

To load thermodynamic constants for TiO2, rutile.

modules.name: thermochem.burcat
documentation:
This module extracts the information provided in *Third Millennium Ideal Gas
and Condensed Phase Thermochemical Database for Combustion with Updates from
Active Thermochemical Tables* by A. Burcat and B. Ruscic. It needs the actual
database BURCAT_THR.xml to run, which is already included in the thermochem
library.
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: thermochem.burcat.Element

documentation: Density in kg/m³.

documentation: Calculates the specific heat capacity in J/mol K

methods.name: thermochem.burcat.Element.cp_
documentation: Computes the specific heat capacity in J/kg K for a given temperature

methods.name: thermochem.burcat.Element.cp
documentation: Computes the specific heat capacity in J/kg K at 298 K (Reference T)

methods.name: thermochem.burcat.Element.ho

documentation: Computes the total enthalpy in J/kg

documentation: Computes entropy in J/mol K

methods.name: thermochem.burcat.Element.go
documentation:
Computes the Gibbs free energy from the sensible enthalpy in
J/mol

This is a helper class.  It is intended to be created via an
Elementdb object but it can be used on its own. Take a look at
Elementdb class for example usage.

Units are in standard units: K, J, kg. Conversion functions are provided in
the external units module.

One extra feature not explained in Elementdb documentation is that
it contains the number of each atom, useful for computing chemical
reactions.

```pycon
>>> db = Elementdb()
>>> weird = db.getelementdata("C8H6O2")
>>> print(weird.elements)
[('C', 8), ('H', 6), ('O', 2)]
```

classes.name: thermochem.burcat.Mixture

methods.name: thermochem.burcat.Mixture.__iter__
documentation: The following functions are an iterator. Its purpose is to be able to
iterate through all the elements of a mix.

methods.name: thermochem.burcat.Mixture.add
documentation: Add a component to the mixture

methods.name: thermochem.burcat.Mixture.delete
documentation: Delete a formula from the mixture

methods.name: thermochem.burcat.Mixture.mm
documentation:
Computes the equivalent molar mass for a mix

$$
M_m = \frac{1}{N_m} \sum_i N_i M_i
$$

methods.name: thermochem.burcat.Mixture.density
documentation:
Computes the density for a given mix of gases in kg/m³

The equivalent R for a mix is $R_m = \frac{R_u}{M_n}$,
where $M_n$ is the equivalent molar mass for the mix.

methods.name: thermochem.burcat.Mixture.extensive
documentation:
Computes the extensive value for a mix. Remember that an extensive
value depends on the amount of matter. Enthalpy and volume are
extensive values.

$$
ext = \frac{1}{N_m M_m} \sum_i N_i M_i ext_i
$$

methods.name: thermochem.burcat.Mixture.cp_
documentation: Computes the heat capacity at a given temperature in J/kg K.

methods.name: thermochem.burcat.Mixture.cp
documentation:
Computes the heat capacity at room temperature, 298.15K.
Results in J/kg K.

methods.name: thermochem.burcat.Mixture.ho
documentation: Estimate the sensible enthalpy of the mixture in J/mol.

methods.name: thermochem.burcat.Mixture.h
documentation: Estimate the total enthalpy of the mixture in J/kg.

methods.name: thermochem.burcat.Mixture.so
documentation: Estimate the entropy of the mixture in J/mol K.

methods.name: thermochem.burcat.Mixture.go
documentation:
Estimate the Gibbs free energy using the sensible enthalpy of the
mixture in J/mol.

Class that models a gas mixture. Currently, only volume (molar)
compositions are supported.

You can iterate through all its elements. The item returned is a tuple
containing the element and the amount.

```pycon
>>> db = Elementdb()
>>> mix = db.getmixturedata([("O2 REF ELEMENT", 20.9476),    ("N2  REF ELEMENT", 78.084),    ("CO2", 0.0319),    ("AR REF ELEMENT", 0.9365),    ])
>>> mix_list = [(e[0], round(e[1], 6)) for e in mix]
>>> for e in mix_list: print(e)
(<element> O2 REF ELEMENT, 20.9476)
(<element> N2  REF ELEMENT, 78.084)
(<element> CO2, 0.0319)
(<element> AR REF ELEMENT, 0.9365)
```

You can get elements either by index or by value.

```pycon
>>> print(mix['CO2'])
(<element> CO2, 0.0319)
```

You can also delete components of a mixture. Needed by the MoistAir class

```pycon
>>> mix.delete('CO2')
>>> print(mix)
<Mixture>:
    O2 REF ELEMENT at 20.9476
    N2  REF ELEMENT at 78.084
    AR REF ELEMENT at 0.9365
```

classes.name: thermochem.burcat.Elementdb

methods.name: thermochem.burcat.Elementdb.__init__
documentation:
The database file is read when the class is instantiated.
This is terribly slow as the database is more than 2MB.
Create the instance and the elements at boot, otherwise be
prepared to face huge computation times.

methods.name: thermochem.burcat.Elementdb.search
documentation:
List all the species containing a string. Helpful for
interactive use of the database.

methods.name: thermochem.burcat.Elementdb.getelementdata
documentation: Returns an element instance given the name of the element.

methods.name: thermochem.burcat.Elementdb.getmixturedata
documentation:
Creates a mixture of components given a list of tuples
containing the formula and the volume percent

Class that reads the Alexander Burcat's thermochemical database
for combustion.

```pycon
>>> db = Elementdb()
>>> oxygen = db.getelementdata("O2 REF ELEMENT")
>>> print(oxygen)
<element> O2 REF ELEMENT
>>> print('molar mass', oxygen.mm)
molar mass 0.0319988
>>> print('heat capacity', round(oxygen.cp, 6))
heat capacity 918.078952
```

The reference temperature for enthalpy is 298.15 K

```pycon
>>> print('entropy', round(oxygen.so(298), 6))
entropy 205.133746
>>> print('gibbs free energy', round(oxygen.go(298), 6))
gibbs free energy -61134.262901
```

There's a search function. It is very useful because some names
are a bit tricky. Well, not this one.

```pycon
>>> db.search("AIR")
['AIR']
>>> air = db.getelementdata("AIR")
>>> print('air molar mass', air.mm)
air molar mass 0.02896518
>>> print('heat capacity', round(air.cp, 6))
heat capacity 1004.776251
>>> print(round(air.density(101325, 298), 6))
1.184519
```

The element database can create also mixtures.  It returns an
instance of Mixture object that can give you the same as the
Element class for any mixture.

```pycon
>>> mix = db.getmixturedata([("O2 REF ELEMENT", 20.9476),    ("N2  REF ELEMENT", 78.084),    ("CO2", 0.0319),    ("AR REF ELEMENT", 0.9365),    ])
>>> print(mix)
<Mixture>:
    O2 REF ELEMENT at 20.9476
    N2  REF ELEMENT at 78.084
    CO2 at 0.0319
    AR REF ELEMENT at 0.9365
>>> print(round(mix.cp, 6))
1004.722171
>>> print(round(mix.mm, 6))
0.028965
```
